coredata.dc:title: High performance of electrochemical and fluorescent probe by interaction of cell and bacteria with pH-sensitive polymer dots coated surfaces
coredata.dc:creator: Phuong, Pham Thi My
Ryplida, Benny
In, Insik
Park, Sung Young
coredata.dc:description: Using pH-switchable fluorescent polymer dots (PD) by means of fluorescent, colorimetric, and electrochemical signals generated from surfaces coated with PD of zwitterionic structure provided a fast and easy method to assess their performance in mammalian cell and bacterial interactions. The PD-coated surfaces showed high sensitivity over a broad range of pH levels by switching reversibly zwitterionic states, which led to an excellent cellular resistance effect by inhibiting the attachment of nearly 95% of mammalian cells. Similarly, they exhibited a strong interaction with the negatively charged surfaces of bacteria, as observed in the fluorescence ON/OFF system. In addition, PD were employed to detect the attachment of mammalian and bacterial cells: we deposited PD on a screen-printed carbon electrode for cyclic voltammetry analysis. Notably, the presence of cells remarkably interfered with the current flow between the PD and the screen-printed carbon electrode surface by causing an impressive decline in both reduction-oxidation signals, implying the high sensitivity of the PD-coated surfaces to cells and bacteria in different pH environments. Therefore, as smart materials with high sensitivity, biocompatibility, selectivity, and accuracy, PD-coated surfaces represent a promising approach to visualizing and controlling biological cell attachment, thereby helping to avoid contamination in biomedical applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Cell interaction
Fluorescence
Electrochemical sensor
Polymer dots
Zwitterion

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: absattachment articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref specialabst

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Carbonization of catechol-quaternized poly(ethylene glycol) grafted poly(sulfobetaine methacrylate) (PD)
Preparation for surface coating with PD
Cell-surface interaction studies by fluorescent, colorimetric, and CV analyses

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: Ministry of Trade, Industry and Energy

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by Grant No. 10062079 from the Ministry of Trade, Industry & Energy ( MOTIE ) and the Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF), funded by the Ministry of Education ( NRF-2017R1A2B2002365 and 2018R1A6A1A03023788 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) 1H NMR analysis of PD, (b) UV–Vis spectrum of the PD at different pH levels, (c) TEM image and corresponding lattice d-spacing, (d) hydrodynamic size of the particles, and (e) fluorescence spectra of PD at pH 6.0, 7.4, and 9.0 (0.5 mg/mL) (Excitation wavelength: 400 nm).

(a) Water static contact angle (deg) analyses of bare and PD-coated substrates before and after exposure to different pH levels. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) survey spectra of PD-coated PET: (b) wide scan and narrow scan of (c) C 1s (d) S 2p peaks.

(a) Hydrophilic–hydrophobic reversibility of PD-coated PET substrates and (b) fluorescent behavior of PD-coated PET at different excitation wavelengths (405, 488, and 543 nm, respectively) under different pH conditions.

In vitro HeLa cell adhesion test for unmodified and PD-coated surfaces at various pH levels: (a) Confocal images of the modified PET surface at different excitation wavelengths and (b) quantitative analysis of cell attachment (%) to various PD-coated surfaces. Cyclic voltammogram of PD-modified SPCEs (c) before and (d) after cell adhesion. Pristine surfaces were used as controls.

In vitro bacterial adhesion test: Confocal and SEM images (scale bar: 10 μm) of PD-coated surface with (a) S. aureus and (c) E. coli. Cyclic voltammogram of PD-modified SPCEs after (b) S. aureus and (d) E. coli adhesion.

Illustration of (a) the synthesis of pH-switchable PD based on zwitterionic polymer CA-PgS, (b) the performance of PD in aqueous and PD-coated surface against cells and bacteria at different pH levels based on fluorescence and electrochemical signals.

head.ce:title: High performance of electrochemical and fluorescent probe by interaction of cell and bacteria with pH-sensitive polymer dots coated surfaces

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of IT Convergence, Korea National University of Transportation, Chungju 380-702, Republic of Korea

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering, Korea National University of Transportation, Chungju 380-702, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemical and Biological Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Polymer Science and Engineering, Korea National University of Transportation, Chungju 380-702, Republic of Korea

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Polymer Science and Engineering

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Department of IT Convergence, Korea National University of Transportation, Chungju 380-702, Republic of Korea.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Using pH-switchable fluorescent polymer dots (PD) by means of fluorescent, colorimetric, and electrochemical signals generated from surfaces coated with PD of zwitterionic structure provided a fast and easy method to assess their performance in mammalian cell and bacterial interactions. The PD-coated surfaces showed high sensitivity over a broad range of pH levels by switching reversibly zwitterionic states, which led to an excellent cellular resistance effect by inhibiting the attachment of nearly 95% of mammalian cells. Similarly, they exhibited a strong interaction with the negatively charged surfaces of bacteria, as observed in the fluorescence ON/OFF system. In addition, PD were employed to detect the attachment of mammalian and bacterial cells: we deposited PD on a screen-printed carbon electrode for cyclic voltammetry analysis. Notably, the presence of cells remarkably interfered with the current flow between the PD and the screen-printed carbon electrode surface by causing an impressive decline in both reduction-oxidation signals, implying the high sensitivity of the PD-coated surfaces to cells and bacteria in different pH environments. Therefore, as smart materials with high sensitivity, biocompatibility, selectivity, and accuracy, PD-coated surfaces represent a promising approach to visualizing and controlling biological cell attachment, thereby helping to avoid contamination in biomedical applications.

pH-switchable fluorescent polymer dots coated surfaces for cells/bacteria detection via fluorescence ON/OFF, colorimetric and electrochemical signals.
Unlabelled Image

ce:section.ce:para: The attachment of cells and bacteria to surfaces is the main cause of biological contamination in biomedical devices [1,2]. Numerous studies have been conducted to synthesize and characterize anti- or low cell adhesion materials. However, the ability to visualize cell adhesion to surfaces has not yet been investigated because the structure is unstable and hard to control [3,4]. There are several methods to immobilize antifouling polymers on a variety of substrates: physically, by spin-coating or dip-coating, and chemically, by covalent grafting. However, the amount of materials that can be tethered to the substrate is limited; this shortcoming can affect the efficiency of the anti-adhesion effect. Therefore, it is necessary to control cell binding to surfaces through functionalization with fluorescent polymer dots (PD), which possess unique photoluminescent properties. Such nanoparticles have been applied to cell culture studies as fluorescent probes in chemical sensors, biological regulation, and other related fields, via the quenching effect of the electron transfer process [5–7].

Recently, PD have become the most impressive candidate for many applications because of their nanoscale size and unique chemical and optical properties, such as tunable sensitivity, biocompatibility, environmental friendliness, and stability [8,9]. Based on the fluorescence-quenching properties of PD in a fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) system, previous studies on PD inclusion of PD that effectively improved the efficiency and performance of devices by protecting from biological attachment or by serving as bacterial biosensors, among other relevant applications [10,11]. Additionally, biofilm formation and biological adhesion can be detected by electrochemical techniques, especially cyclic voltammetry (CV): observing the current change in a cyclic voltammogram based on the electron transfer between a bacterial and an electrode surface provides a highly accurate method for monitoring cell–surface interactions [12].

Researchers have successfully identified a number of low- and non-fouling materials, owing to common features such as a hydrophilic nature, electrical neutrality, and hydrogen bond acceptors and/or donors [13–16]. Above all, zwitterionic polymers have proven to be the best alternative, with impressive antifouling properties [17]. Several studies have shown various derivatives of conventional zwitterionic polymers that can switch between a zwitterionic and a non-zwitterionic form [18]. These surface charge transitions with respect to pH allow the surface to be switched from a cell-attractive to a cell-resistant state depending on hydrophobic–hydrophilic interactions. Prior research has also developed a zwitterionic polymer to detect the presence of bacteria based on ionic-complex formation from opposite charges between bacterial surfaces and the zwitterionic backbone during pH variations [19]. Tethering highly hydrophilic materials onto hydrophobic substrates is problematic; this is the most common drawback of zwitterionic materials for coating purposes. This issue has typically been solved by introducing an adhesive linker that helps the polymer anchor to a surface for extended periods of time. One of the popular adhesive compounds is catechol: a side chain of a particular amino acid of dopamine secreted by mussels. Overcoming the drawback of previously known linkers, catechol reportedly acts as a strongly adhesive compound on diverse inorganic and polymeric substrates based on catechol chemistry under alkaline conditions [20,21].

The rapid detection of cellular and bacterial presence on a surface can be easily implemented by coating a pH-sensitive carbonized polymer dot with a transitioning hydrophilic–hydrophobic property that responds to surrounding pH conditions onto the surface utilizing catechol moieties. Overcoming the recent drawbacks of conventional, longstanding techniques such as SEM images for surface-sensing cells, a pH-switchable zwitterionic PD-coated surface is proposed here as a fast and efficient method to detect and control the biological adhesion of cells and bacteria in accordance with fluorescent ON/OFF phenomena, with changes in the electrochemical signals of the generated current from cyclic voltammetry.

Polyethylene glycol (PEG; Mw 3500), 2-dimethylaminoethyl methacrylate (DMA), t-butyl peroxybenzoate, Trizma base, 1,3-propanesultone, 2-chloro-3′,4′-dihydroxyacetonephenone (CA, catechol), concentrated H2SO4, 3-(4,5-dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT), potassium ferricyanide K3[Fe(CN)6], potassium chloride (KCl), MRS, Lysogeny broth (LB), agar, D2O, CDCl3 reagents, deionized water (DDW), glutaraldehyde, anhydrous tetrahydrofuran (THF), toluene, ethanol, diethyl ether, and hexane were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (St. Louis, MO, USA). Strains of the bacteria Escherichia coli (E. coli) and Staphylococcus aureus (S. aureus) and the mammalian HeLa cancerous cell line were obtained from Korean Cell Line Bank (Seoul, Korea). Penicillin-streptomycin, fetal bovine serum (FBS), 0.25% (w/v) Trypsin, 0.03% (w/v) EDTA solution, RPMI 1640 medium, and phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) were purchased from Gibco BRL (Carlsbad, CA, USA). Solid substrates, namely silicon wafer (Si), poly (ethylene terephthalate) (PET), polyvinylchloride (PVC), and polypropylene (PP), were prepared as previously described [10].

The PD were characterized by proton nuclear magnetic resonance (1H NMR), ultraviolet-visible (UV–Vis) spectroscopy, and photoluminescence (PL). Spectra were recorded with a Bruker Advance 400 MHz spectrometer using deuterium oxide (D2O) and chloroform‑d (CDCl3) as solvents, an Optizen 2020UV (Mecasys Co., Ltd., South Korea), and a L550B luminescence spectrometer (Perkin Elmer, Waltham, MA, USA), respectively. Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR) was recorded using a Nicolet 380 FTIR spectrometer (Thermo Scientific, Waltham, MA, USA). X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) spectra were obtained with an XRD Bruker AXS D8 ADVANCE X-ray Diffractometer using Cu Kα radiation at a wavelength of 1.5406 Å. PD particle sizes were measured by dynamic light scattering (DLS; Zetasizer Nano, Malvern Germany), and surface morphology by transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100F, JEOL, Akishima, Tokyo, Japan) at an operating voltage of 200 kV. Surface coating with PD was confirmed by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) using an Omicrometer Escalab (Omicrometer, Germany), and static water contact angles were measured on a DO3210 instrument (KRUSS DSA 100 Optima system, KRUSS, Hamburg, Germany). Fluorescence microscopy images of the coated substrates under different excitation conditions before and after cell adhesion were captured with a LSM510 Confocal Laser-scanning Microscope (CLSM; Carl Zeiss, Germany) equipped with a 364-nm UV laser and 543-nm He/Ne laser. Field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM) micrographs were obtained using a SEM/EDX (JSM-6700F, JEOL, Musashino, Akishima, Tokyo, Japan). Screen-printed carbon electrodes (SPCEs) (ref 110) were purchased from EGTechnology, Korea, and were used for CV analysis using a μStat 4000P Multi Potentiostat (DROPSENS, Spain) consisting of a 4-cm diameter carbon-based working electrode, carbon auxiliary electrode, and silver reference electrode. Zeta-potential data were obtained using a Zeta-potential & Particle size Analyzer (ELSZ; Otsuka Electronics, Japan).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbonization of catechol-quaternized poly(ethylene glycol) grafted poly(sulfobetaine methacrylate) (PD)
ce:section.ce:para: The catechol-quaternized poly(ethylene glycol)-g-poly(2-dimethylaminoethyl methacrylate) (CA-PgP, Mn: 25,800 g/mol) was obtained by a method similar to the one described in our previous report [22]. Subsequently, a zwitterionic polymer (CA-PgS, Mn: 37,560 g/mol) was prepared by betainization of sultone into CA-PgP according to another study [19]. Finally, PD was obtained through a carbonization process by the dropwise addition of 10 mL concentrated H2SO4 into 5 mL solution of CA-PgS in DDW, then allowing for a 1-min reaction with continuous magnetic stirring. The crude product was purified by dialysis and then freeze-dried for 1 day. All the synthesized polymers and PD were confirmed by 1H NMR and UV–Vis spectroscopy.

The 1H NMR (400 MHz, D2O, δ) chemical shifts (in ppm) were as follows: 2.25–2.5 (2H, –CH2– group of DMA), 2.2–2.4 (2H, –CH2– of sultone), 2.8–3.0 (2H, –CH2-SO3− of sultone), 3.1–3.2 [6H, −(CH3)2− of DMA], 3.6 (CH2-CH2O– of PEG), 3.6–3.8 (2H, –CH2-N+R2− of DMA), 4.3–4.5 (2H, –OCH2– of DMA), and 6.67–7.53 (aromatic protons of catechol).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation for surface coating with PD
ce:section.ce:para: PD was coated onto versatile substrates (Si wafer, PET, PVC, PP) via dip coating in TBS (pH 8.5) at room temperature. After 24 h incubation, the coated substrates were rinsed extensively with DDW and dried under an argon stream for future use. To study their pH-responsiveness, the coated surfaces were treated with acidic, basic, and neutral solutions. The coated surfaces before and after pH treatments were characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and static water contact angle. The reversibility between hydrophobicity and hydrophilicity of the coated surfaces was also assessed. Further, the fluorescence of PD-coated PET surfaces was observed by fluorescence microscopy at different excitation wavelengths (405, 488, and 543 nm respectively).

To investigate the interaction between PD-coated surfaces and cells, HeLa cells were grown in a culture flask. After several days of growth, the cells were washed with PBS and detached by 0.03% (w/v) EDTA solution for later use. The acid-treated, base-treated, and neutral-pH-treated PD-coated and the pristine PET substrates were immersed in 2 mL of PBS followed by the addition of 2 mL of the RPMI 1640 medium supplemented with 10% FBS and 1% penicillin-streptomycin. The substrates were then incubated for 1 h at 37 °C in a 5% CO2 incubator. After that, the medium was removed and 2 mL of cell solution (concentration 1 × 106 cells/mL) were subsequently added, with incubation for another 24 h. pH of the cell medium was adjusted to the desired values before the cell attachment assay was performed. Finally, the solution was discarded, and the substrates were washed with PBS several times. The cells adhered onto the substrates were fixed by incubation with 4% formaldehyde for 1 h. The non-adherent cells were removed by suction, followed by washing with PBS. The total number of adherent cells on the substrates was determined, while the cell attachment behavior was examined under the LSM510 confocal laser-scanning microscope at two excitation wavelengths (405 and 488 nm).

S. aureus (gram-positive, strain ATCC 25323) and E. coli (gram negative, strain ATCC 25922) were used to evaluate the binding characteristics of the polymer coatings toward bacteria. S. aureus and E. coli were cultured in MRS and LB broth (50 mL), respectively, and incubated for 24 h at 37 °C. Then, the pH-treated PD-coated PET films were immersed in a suspension of each bacterial strain at a concentration of 108 cells/mL and incubated at 37 °C for 4 h. Next, those substrates were removed from the bacterial suspensions and washed several times with PBS. The bacteria that adhered to the surfaces were fixed with 2.5% glutaraldehyde in PBS, followed by stepwise dehydration in a graded series of ethanol solutions (25%, 50%, 75%, and 100%) and air-drying. Their fluorescent properties were measured using the LSM510 confocal laser scanning microscope (excitation wavelength 405 nm), and the cells were counted based on micrographs from field-emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM).

Commercial SPCE strips were modified with the synthesized PD by a simple drop-coating method. PD solutions of different pH levels were prepared in Tris-buffered saline (TBS). Specifically, 10 μL of the prepared PD dispersion (1 mg/mL) was dropped onto the surface of the working electrode of the SPCE. Then, those SPCEs were dried in an oven at 90 °C for 10 min. Next, the SPCEs were incubated in a HeLa suspension or bacterial solution for 24 h at the same concentrations and conditions as in the previous experiments. The SPCEs were then washed with PBS and dried at room temperature. The PD-altered SPCEs were characterized by CV using a μStat 4000P Multi Potentiostat with 10 mM K3[(FeCN)6] as an electrolyte and 100 mM KCl as a supporting electrolyte. CV curves were scanned in the range from −0.2 to +0.7 V at a scan rate of 50 mV/s.
An MTT assay was carried out to examine the toxicity of the synthesized PD toward both normal and abnormal cells by a method similar to that reported previously [23].

In this study, we investigated the fluorescence ON/OFF system of pH-switchable PD from carbonized zwitterionic polymer CA-PgS, which are coatable on various surfaces owing to the presence of catechol residues. PD exists as core-shell structure in aqueous solution due to the presence of both hydrophobic and hydrophilic segments of partially carbonized polymer with lattice structure as core and PEG and zwitterion as shell, respectively. The aggregation behavior in the core part affected the size change of PD in different pHs. In the solid state, PD exists in a collapsed conformation due to the association of anionic sulfonate moieties and cationic ammonium groups at pH 7.4 that quenched emission of PD on the coated surface. However, the abundance of H+/OH+ ions in pH treated solution disrupted electrostatic interactions between ionic groups that PD-coated surface exhibited stretched conformation, resulting in the increase of net surface charge at pH 6.0 and 9.0, performed as fluorescent “ON” behavior. The surfaces coated with PD showed an excellent response at various pH levels and revealed effective resistance to cellular attachment as well as ionic interactions with the bacterial surface. Specifically, in acidic and basic environments, PD-coated surfaces exhibited an excellent anti-cell adhesion effect by preventing mammalian cell attachment, observed by strong fluorescent intensities. On the other hand, the association of the negative sulfonate end group with the positively charged ammonium group formed on the coated surface at pH-neutrality enhanced the hydrophobicity by aggregation that attracted the adhesion of cells, causing fluorescence quenching. These interactions could be identified rapidly and with high accuracy by analyzing the fluorescent behavior and current signal generated by CV. The PD-coated surface has thus shown its potential as a cell attachment imaging and detection tool for preventing contamination in biomedical applications (Scheme 1 ).

The structure of the PD system was characterized by 1H NMR spectroscopy (Fig. 1a). The appearance of the peaks at approximately 2.2–3.0 and 7.5 ppm represented the successful synthesis of CA-PgS containing sultone and catechol groups, respectively. Furthermore, 1H NMR spectra obtained after the carbonization process showed similar peaks when compared to the spectra acquired before carbonization (Fig. S1), thus revealing the remaining structure of the material when it was going through the dehydration process. Additionally, the integration of the catechol group was observed by UV–Vis absorbance measurements. As shown in Fig. 1b, catechol conjugation was confirmed in the UV–Vis absorption spectrum by peaks at 280 and 360 nm. Moreover, the UV–Vis spectra of PD in TBS at different pH levels showed no differences, indicating the stability of our PD structure at various pH levels. Nevertheless, a small red shift in the pH 9.0 spectrum could be explained by a minor enhancement of π-π bonds resulting from the auto-oxidation of catechol groups at alkaline pH [24].

To elucidate the interplanar distance of PD, a XRD measurement was examined that showed a broad, less intense peak of PD (002), with d-spacing of 0.23 nm at around 23.5°; it indicated the partial oxidation of the polymer by strong acids to generate carbonized nanoparticles of a smaller size, as depicted in Fig. S7 [25]. Moreover, a TEM image with the size distribution histogram of PD demonstrated the average particle size to be 150 nm (Fig. S8). After the carbonizing acid-catalyzed process, TEM images showed round-shaped particles with d-lattice separations (approximately 0.27–0.29 nm), indicative of the graphene carbon structure, as shown in Fig. 1c [26]. In this work, concentrated H2SO4 was used as a dehydrating catalyst in the carbonization process to create the partial lattice fringe structure of the zwitterionic polymer at 1 min of reaction. During the oxidative process, abundant oxygen-containing functional groups such as hydroxyl (–OH) and carboxyl (–COOH) were introduced to the PD surface, which was essential to their tunable emission [27–29]. Due to the presence of both hydrophobic (partially carbonized polymer with lattice structure) and hydrophilic (PEG and zwitterion) segments, PD will form a core-shell structure in aqueous solution. At pH 7.4, the shell layer containing the PEG segment could maintain its structure while in acidic and basic conditions, with the zwitterion moieties also present in the shell, contributing to the control of the dispersity of the nanoparticles in varied pH conditions. The fluorescent on/off behavior related electrostatic interactions has been proven by changes in the hydrodynamic particle size of the synthesized PD (Fig. 1d) at different pH conditions. Specifically, in acidic environments, the cationic form of PD was formed due to the protonation of the ammonium and sulfobetaine groups, and the particle size was found to be ~74.47 nm ± 1.8 nm, resulting in a strong blue fluorescence intensity (excitation wavelength 400 nm; Fig. 1e). The PD particle size was approximately 195.43 nm ± 2.2 nm at pH 7.4, and fluorescence was quenched. This result confirmed the formation of electrostatic interactions yielding aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ) and π-π stackings of PD that caused more aggregation along with partial auto-polymerization of catechol groups, demonstrating much bigger hydrodynamic size [30,31]. In basic environments, the anionic form of PD was confirmed by the ~ 83.52 nm ± 1.2 nm particle size in DLS, which proved the interruption of those electrostatic interactions by the redundancy of hydroxyl ions. However, the fluorescence of PD was almost quenched in basic solution. This effect is caused by the polydopamine aggregation in aqueous solution, owing to the auto-polymerization of catechol residues under alkaline conditions and resulting in an effective fluorescence-quenching ability [32]. As shown in Scheme 2, the change of particle sizes was contributed by the aggregation behavior in the core part that including zwitterion and catechol moieties, which affect the size change of PD in different pH in aqueous condition. PEG in the shell has ability to provide colloidal stability and biocompatibility for the PD, providing steric stabilization and a short-range repulsive hydration layer around the particles that imparts an excellent long-term stability in extreme pH [33,34]. Overall, the results indicate that the synthesized PD are highly influenced by pH and are suitable for coating a surface due to the presence of catechol groups.

By a simple dip-coating method, the PD were coated to Si wafer and polymeric surfaces: PET, PVC, and PP (Scheme 1b). The wettability of our PD and diverse substrates were studied by static water contact angle measurement. As shown in Fig. 2a, a decrease in contact angles was observed on PD-coated surfaces after coating and acidic/basic treatments, indicating that our nanomaterials were more hydrophilic than the versatile polymeric surfaces, owing to the quaternary ammonium from the DMA residue and the sulfobetaine residues from the zwitterionic conformation, improving the wettability of the coated surfaces [35]. A slight increase in the contact angle on the Si wafer surface revealed that the synthesized PD coating material was less hydrophilic than the bare Si wafer surface. On the other hand, the contact angle of all PD-coated surfaces measured after treatment with TBS pH 7.4 showed a reduction in hydrophilicity due to the ionic complex formed at pH neutrality between opposite charge residues on the surface. This further supported the ionic interaction between ionized zwitterionic chains and other charged residues on the surface.

XPS analysis was conducted for an elemental analysis of the PD-coated PET substrate. As shown in Fig. 2b and Table S1, the presence of four peaks in a wide-scan, for C 1s (65.94%), O 1s (31.33%), N 1s (1.26%), and S 2p (1.47%), pointed to the successful adhesion of our material to the PET substrate. The C 1s core-line spectrum of the materials can be curve-fitted with five component peaks with binding energies of approximately 284.5, 283, 287.5, 286.5, and 285.5 eV, attributed to the CC, CC, OCO, CN, and CS linkages, respectively. The large amount of CC bonding revealed the presence of many π-π bonds in the PD structure following dehydration (Fig. 2c). In addition, the S 2p XPS signal at 168.8 eV pointed to –SO3H groups of sultone moieties, as displayed in Fig. 2d. The narrow scan of the O 1s spectrum, provided in Fig. S9a, showed peaks at 531.1 and 533.1 eV, corresponding to the CO and CO bonds of PD. Further, two peaks at 400.8 and 401.7 in the narrow scan of the N 1s spectrum confirmed the existence of tertiary and quaternary nitrogen from DMA moieties, as shown in Fig. S9b [10,35]. In the FTIR spectrum (Fig. S10), the presence of sulfobetaine segments could be confirmed from the sulfonate and ester carbonyl groups observed in the bands of the –SO stretch at 1040 cm−1 and 1180 cm−1, the –C–S stretch at 606 cm−1, the –C–N+ stretch at 1640 cm−1, and the O–CO stretch at 1720 cm−1, respectively [36]. Following sensing, the cells were removed from the coated surfaces, which subsequently showed unchanged elemental compositions. The results of the FTIR and narrow-scan XPS spectra of C 1s, O 1s, N 1s, and S 2p confirmed the zwitterionic structure of PD after the carbonization process.

The hydrophobic–hydrophilic reversibility of the coated PET surfaces was evaluated based on the contact angle with pH while adjusting the surrounding environments from acidic to basic. As shown in Fig. 3a, the PD-coated PET was hydrophilic at pH 6.0; the contact angle then increased at pH 7.4, and it reverted to the hydrophilic state at pH 9.0 to finish one cycle. This was due to the transformation and interactions between the zwitterions of ammonium and sultone residues during the pH change. In the solid state, PD exists in a collapsed conformation due to the association of anionic sulfonate end groups and positive charge of ammonium groups at physiological pH of 7.4 that quenched emission of PD on the coated surface. However, in the presence of abundant H+/OH+ ions in pH treated solution, PD coated on the surface exhibited stretched conformation due to the disruption electrostatic interactions between ionic groups that resulted in the increase of net surface charge at pH 6.0 and 9.0, subsequently performed fluorescent “ON” behavior [37]. Due to the ON/OFF fluorescent behavior of our PD over the wide pH range of the aqueous media, the PD-coated PET films were observed under a fluorescence microscope at different excitation wavelengths (405, 488, and 543 nm) after treatments with TBS at pH 6.0, 7.4, and 9.0, which showed different colors (blue, green, and red respectively; Fig. 3b), in accordance with the multicolor fluorescence property of the PD, as shown in Fig. S2. The strong fluorescent behavior was observed on both acid- and base-treated surfaces, whereas the fluorescence on the neutral-pH-treated surface was almost quenched. This quenching phenomenon was initiated by the formation of the ionic complex among opposite charge residues of interacting zwitterions on the surface. Additionally, strong fluorescent ON signals were observed on PD-coated surfaces after treatment at pH 4.0 and pH 12.0, as shown in Fig. S6, indicating that the ON/OFF PL behavior of PD-coated surfaces could be applied over a wide range of pH levels. All the above results strongly indicated that the surfaces coated with our nanomaterial could serve as smart surfaces in various fields such as antifouling and tissue engineering. Furthermore, we have compared the fluorescent properties in both aqueous and solid state of PD- and CA-PgS as shown in Fig. S11. The PD was coated onto the surface resulted in a strong fluorescent signal, which offers the ability for antifouling study via fluorescent changes when interacted with cells, compared to CA-PgS coated surface.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Cell-surface interaction studies by fluorescent, colorimetric, and CV analyses
ce:section.ce:para: The switchable pH-responsiveness of the PD made the fluorescence-based interactions between the modified surfaces and cells worth examining. Here, the PD-coated surfaces were used for a cell-surface adhesion study with HeLa cells. After 24 h of incubation with HeLa cell solution, acid- and base-treated modified PET surfaces showed high resistance to cell attachment, whereas neutral pH-treated surfaces did not. Due to the fluorescent properties of the PD-coated surfaces, we applied them to the culture medium and observed the cells by confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM), as shown in Fig. 4a. When exposed to the cell suspension, the pristine PET surface serving as a control showed a high binding affinity for HeLa cells. However, due to hydrophobic interactions, cells also tended to attach to the PD-coated PET surfaces that had been treated with the pH 7.4 solution [38], which caused fluorescence quenching at different excitation wavelengths. On the other hand, the PD-coated PET surfaces treated at pH 6.0 and pH 9.0 showed high resistance to HeLa cell attachment, retaining bright fluorescence under the microscope. Further quantitative analysis of the cellular adhesion on PET and other types of coated substrates revealed excellent inhibition of HeLa cell attachment to the surfaces treated with acidic and basic solutions, whereas cells tended to adhere to the neutral-pH-treated surfaces, as presented in Fig. 4b. To investigate the cell attachment, we evaluated the surface roughness using microscopic images with the 3D surface plot, which illustrated the change in roughness in the presence of cells (Fig. S13). It can be clarified that the PD changed from a hydrophobic to a hydrophilic state in the 6.0 to 9.0 pH range, which means that nanoparticles are activated in acidic and basic environments and deactivated at neutral pH. At pH 6.0 and 9.0, the nanoparticles were in a hydrophilic zwitterionic state and formed a hydration layer to prevent cell adhesion. By contrast, at neutral pH, the ionic interaction between opposite charges raised the hydrophobicity of the PD-coated surface, where hydrophobic interaction led to cell adhesion [19].

CV can be a selective and accurate detection method for evaluating the adhesion of cells on modified surfaces based on redox electrochemical signals generated by an applied voltage [39,40]. Hence, in this study, we first tried to apply the CV sensitivity of the PD-coated surface toward mammalian HeLa cells. As illustrated in Fig. 4c, depositing our nanomaterial onto SPCE sensor strips enhanced the overall electrical conductivity of the SPCE in reversible electrochemical reactions occurring on the working electrode when compared with the cyclic voltammogram of an unmodified SPCE strip, at all pH levels. Subsequently, the modified, post-treatment SPCE strips were incubated with HeLa cells for 24 h, followed by washing steps to remove non-adherent or loosely adherent cells from the sensor strip surface. Interestingly, after incubation, the acid- and base-treated PD-deposited SPCE strips showed an insignificant change in the cyclic voltammogram, indicating that the PD-coated SPCEs retained better capacity than the uncoated SPCE sensor strips. In contrast, the inactivated PD-modified SPCE strip manifested a significant decrease in the generated current at pH 7.4, as illustrated in Fig. 4d. This could be explained by the formation of a HeLa cell layer at the surface, which can function as a barrier blocking the electron transfer current flow at the electrode active surface, thereby interfering with the electrochemical reactions there. In the other hand, we have evaluated the antifouling ability of only polymer (CA-PgS) after coating onto surface (Fig. S12). The result showed that the CA-PgS-coated surface exhibited antifouling properties, similarly with its carbonized product (PD). However, the CA-PgS coated surface cannot display different fluorescent signals with low sensitivity of electrochemical sensing to recognize the presence of cells. In terms of sensitivity, the PD-coated surface also demonstrated a higher sensitivity to the presence of cells, compared to bare SPCE strip (Fig. S14). Therefore, the PD-modified SPCE sensor strip was shown to be highly sensitive to the presence of cells at different pH conditions and resistant to cell adhesion in activated states.

It is well known that the bacterial cell surface is negatively charged due to the phosphate groups of teichoic acids linked to either peptidoglycan or the underlying plasma membrane in gram-positive bacteria (S. aureus), and the negatively charged lipopolysaccharides in the outer membrane of gram-negative bacteria (E. coli) [41]. We next studied the behavior of our nanomaterials toward bacterial adhesion due to the potential ionic complex interaction between the bacterial surface and our material. The bacterial adhesion experiment was conducted by incubating bacteria (suspensions of E. coli or S. aureus strains) with the PD-coated PET surfaces that were pre-treated with solutions of various pH [42]. After 1 h of incubation, all the substrates were washed several times with PBS and observed under CLSM at an excitation wavelength of 405 nm. A previous study has shown that the interaction of zwitterionic materials with bacterial surfaces is represented by changes in fluorescence following pH changes, making it useful for bacterial detection [19]. Similarly, our confocal image findings (Fig. 5a and c) showed the change in fluorescence of our PD-coated surfaces when two species of bacteria were allowed to adhere. Acid-treated surfaces manifested a remarkable decline in fluorescence intensity after adhesion, as shown in Scheme 1, while the fluorescence of neutral-pH-treated surfaces was almost quenched. These phenomena can be explained by the interaction between PD and bacteria, which could be mediated by the formation of ionic complexes between the deprotonated phosphates on the bacterial surface and the positively charged ammonium in the zwitterionic state of PD. In basic conditions, a slight decrease in fluorescence intensity was observed, indicating a small amount of bacterial adherence to the PET film. This might be due to the interaction between the remaining cationic ammonium sides of PD on the coated basic surfaces and the negatively charged bacterial surfaces. Overall, our results strongly indicated that the surfaces coated with PD were highly sensitive to pH influence, which led to surface charge-mediated interactions with the surrounding bacterial cells, as described in Scheme 1b. The measured zeta-potentials of PD under acidic (pH 6.0) and basic (pH 9.0) conditions were +10.42 ± 1.2 eV and −10.43 ± 1.3 eV, respectively, supporting the charged forms of PD. Moreover, at pH 7.4, the coated surface hydrophilicity decreased due to the fluorescence quenching interactions of the zwitterionic structure (zeta-potential +0.42 ± 1.0 eV) and bacteria also bound to this surface through hydrophobic interactions. A higher number of E. coli cells bound to the substrate compared to S. aureus during the zwitterionic state of PD, as illustrated in Fig. S3a and S3b, corresponding to a lower bacterial coverage percentage of the PD-coated substrates at all pH levels. This effect is due to the difference in bacterial cell structure between E. coli and S. aureus. E. coli have a more hydrophobic outer membrane with more negative charges from liposaccharides as compared to the negative charges of teichoic acids in S. aureus [43,44]. In addition, the interaction between our nanomaterials and bacteria at various pH levels was studied by CV at the same time. At pH 6.0 and 7.4, the sensor strips coated with our materials showed a noticeable loss of electrochemical current signals, while an insignificant signal reduction was found at pH 9.0, consistent with the number of bacterial cells attached to the surface. This phenomenon represented the pH-dependent variation in the bacterial cell numbers binding to those surfaces, which determined the surface charge of the PD-coated surfaces due to the potential active surface blockage that could interfere with the redox activities of the electrochemical reactions (Fig. 5b and d) as compared to the electrodes prior to bacterial adhesion (Fig. S4) [45].

To evaluate the biocompatibility of our PD, we examined their toxicity toward two mammalian cell lines, namely MDCK and HeLa cells. After incubation with serial dilutions of our nanomaterial, more than 95% cell viability was achieved at all dilutions. This finding indicated that our material is nontoxic to both normal and abnormal cells (Fig. S5). Therefore, our nanomaterials could serve as biocompatible materials for biomedical applications.

We successfully synthesized surface coating PD for use as an intelligent surface to rapidly visualize the presence of cells and bacteria over a broad range of pH levels. PD confers coated surfaces pH-responsive properties, which were evaluated in cell-surface interaction studies based on fluorescent behavior. Under acidic and basic conditions, our PD showed strong fluorescence intensity, illustrating an efficient inhibition of mammalian cell attachment due to the strong hydrophilic nature of PD. Conversely, at neutral pH, the ionic-complex formation of zwitterions caused a reduction in PD-coated surface hydrophilicity, which quenched fluorescence intensity. The cell detection ability of PD-coated surfaces was subsequently studied by CV, revealing a significant decrease in anodic and cathodic peaks at pH 7.4 because of cell attachment. In contrast, there were no changes under acidic and basic conditions, owing to the high resistance against cell adhesion of the coated surfaces. Hence, it is faster and more convenient to detect and regulate cell attachment by means of fluorescence and CV signals. The changes in electrochemical signals during CV were also attributed to mammalian and bacterial cell adherence onto PD-coated surfaces. Therefore, surfaces coated with our materials hold great promise as cell detectors on highly sensitive surfaces, offering great accuracy, stability based on tunable fluorescence, and electrochemical performance over a broad range of surrounding pH conditions.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by Grant No. 10062079 from the Ministry of Trade, Industry & Energy (MOTIE) and the Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF), funded by the Ministry of Education (NRF-2017R1A2B2002365 and 2018R1A6A1A03023788).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.msec.2019.03.098.
